1.synthesis:
Hiram king of Tyre sends his servants to Solomon when he hears that Solomon has been anointed king in his father’s place; Hiram had always loved David (v. 1). Solomon’s response is a diplomatic letter explaining his project: YHWH gave David no rest from his enemies, so David could not build a house for YHWH’s name. But now YHWH has given Solomon rest on all sides, with no adversary and no misfortune. Solomon intends to build the house for the name of YHWH his God, as YHWH promised David: your son who I will set on your throne shall build the house for my name (vv. 2–5). The request follows: command that cedars from Lebanon be cut for me; my servants will join your servants; I will pay what you fix for wages, for you know that there is no one among us who knows how to cut timber like the Sidonians (v. 6).

Hiram is pleased by the arrangement and sends cedar and cypress logs by sea in rafts, in exchange for twenty thousand cors of wheat and twenty thousand cors of beaten oil for Hiram’s household (vv. 7–11). YHWH gave Solomon wisdom as he promised, and there was peace between Hiram and Solomon; the two made a covenant (v. 12). Then Solomon conscripted forced labor from all Israel — thirty thousand men, sent to Lebanon ten thousand a month in shifts, under the oversight of Adoniram (vv. 13–14). Seventy thousand burden-bearers, eighty thousand quarriers in the hill country, and 3,300 supervisors direct the work of preparing great, costly stones for the temple foundation (vv. 15–18).

The temple preparation narrative frames the building project as the fulfillment of a specific divine commission: YHWH promised David that his son would build the house, and now that son is making arrangements to do exactly that. The inclusion of Hiram — a Phoenician king, not an Israelite — as the indispensable partner for the project reflects the reality that the temple required expertise and materials that Israel’s own tradition could not supply. The building of the house for YHWH’s name was a project that required the wisdom of Israel’s king and the craft of Phoenician woodworkers operating together under treaty. The international cooperation reflects the Solomonic vision of Israel at the center of a network of nations rather than isolated from them.
1.historical_context: Cedar from Lebanon was the premium building material of the ancient Near East; Phoenician craftsmen were the recognized experts in woodworking and maritime transport. The bilateral treaty between Solomon and Hiram was a standard ancient Near Eastern trade agreement: timber and craft labor in exchange for agricultural commodities (wheat and olive oil). The three-shift rotation for the Lebanon work crews (v. 14) was a form of labor conscription (mas) that applied to Israelites — a distinction that would later become significant when 9:20–22 clarifies that Israelites served as soldiers and officials while foreign residents performed the heavy labor. The massive workforce described — thirty thousand Israelite rotational workers, seventy thousand burden-bearers, eighty thousand quarriers — represents an enormous national mobilization for what was essentially a religious building project.
1.literary_structure: 1 Kings 5:1–18 is structured as a diplomatic exchange leading to a building preparation narrative. The letter to Hiram (vv. 2–6) anchors the temple project in the Davidic promise; Hiram’s response (vv. 7–11) provides the covenant theological context — Hiram blessed YHWH who has given David a wise son. The covenant formula in v. 12 — peace between the two kings — frames the commercial relationship theologically. The workforce numbers that follow (vv. 13–18) are the operational implementation of the covenant just made. The chapter is the “preparation” section that sets the stage for the building description in chapter 6.
1.theological_application: Solomon’s grounding of the temple project in the Davidic promise rather than his own initiative models the appropriate relationship between human building projects and divine commissioning. He does not present the temple as his own idea or his own achievement but as the fulfillment of what YHWH promised his father: “your son shall build the house for my name.” The use of international labor and expertise — Hiram’s craftsmen alongside Israelite workers — reflects the principle that the building of a house for YHWH draws on all the capacities available in creation, not only those that belong to the covenant people specifically. The covenant people provide the theological vision and the divine commission; the nations contribute expertise and materials. This pattern will recur in the building of the eschatological city, where “the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it” (Rev 21:24).
1.christology: David could not build the temple because “you have shed so much blood on the earth before me” (1 Chron 22:8); it was reserved for the man of peace whose name would be Shalom. The typological movement from David (the warrior who secured the territory) to Solomon (the wise king who built the house in peace) images the movement from the old covenant (secured through military conflict) to the new covenant (secured by the Prince of Peace). But even Solomon’s peace was partial: the thirty thousand conscripted laborers, the seventy thousand burden-bearers, the forced labor that later split the kingdom — none of this is the rest that the temple was meant to image. The building of the true temple is Christ himself: “destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up” (John 2:19). The house built with conscripted labor was the type; the body raised without human hands is the reality.
